mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz R350 or R500 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M272 engine typically costs £2,500–£5,000 supply only; a reconditioned M113 costs £3,000–£5,500; a reconditioned M273 costs £3,500–£7,500. Used units are available from £1,500–£3,500. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on the workshop. Balance shaft repairs on the M272 cost around £5,000–£7,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your R-Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz R350 / R500 petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which engine. The M113 V8 (2006–2010) is widely regarded as 'one of the most reliable engines I've ever encountered'. The M272 V6 (2006–2010) is the least reliable — balance shaft failure is a class-action-level issue. The M273 V8 (2010–2013) is more powerful but shares the M272's balance shaft/idler gear concerns. Buy the M113 if you can.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz R350 and R500 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: M272: balance shaft failure (soft metal sprocket wears, causing timing issues — P0016–P0019 codes), rough running. M113: oil leaks (valve stem seals, oil pickup O-ring, coolant O-rings), intake manifold flap failure (P2005). M273: balance shaft/idler gear wear (same issue as M272). All models: 7G-Tronic transmission valve body failure.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz R350 / R500 petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: R-Class values range from £3,000–£12,000 depending on condition and spec. A £2,500–£7,500 reconditioned engine is 25–50% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example with good bodywork, replacement is often worthwhile. On a tired example with multiple other issues (Airmatic, transmission), get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz R350 or R500 petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. W251 R350 (2006–2010) uses M272; W251 R500 (2006–2010) uses M113; W251 R500 (2010–2013) uses M273. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M272 balance shaft, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The balance shaft can be replaced — it's a known weak point. Replacement costs £5,000–£7,000. If the balance shaft fails and timing is thrown off, the engine can be destroyed. A preventative balance shaft replacement with the updated timing gear is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£2,500+). At the first sign of rattling or check engine light (P0016–P0019) on a pre-2010 R350, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz R350 / R500 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Budget priority; older M272 or M113 examples

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current R-Class petrol market. Balance shaft repairs on the M272 typically cost $8,000 (£6,000+). The M113 is the most affordable V8 option; the M273 commands a premium. Reconditioned engines from specialist suppliers start from around £2,500 supply only.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an R-Class petrol engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The longitudinal V6/V8 layout makes access relatively straightforward compared to transverse layouts.

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Failure (R350 — 2006–2010)
problems.symptoms: Engine rattle or ticking noise from the front of the engine, check engine light (P0016, P0017, P0018, P0019), rough idling, stalling, rough running
problems.repairCost: £5,000–£7,000 (balance shaft, timing chain and guide replacement)
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£5,000 (if balance shaft failure destroys the engine)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: this is the single biggest risk on the M272 V6

problems.recommendation: The M272's balance shaft sprocket was made from soft metal that wears prematurely, causing timing to be thrown off. A class action lawsuit was filed over this issue. It's been said that "ALL will eventually fail". The repair costs are "huge, $8,000". If you're buying a pre-2010 R350, check the engine number — later engines were revised. If you hear any rattling or see check engine lights (P0016–P0019), book an inspection immediately. A preventative balance shaft replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full balance shaft failure guide

problems.title: M113 Oil Leaks — Valve Stem Seals, Oil Pickup O-Ring, Coolant O-Rings (R500 — 2006–2010)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell, excessive oil consumption, coolant loss, engine knocking, eventual engine failure
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£4,000 (seal replacement — valve stem seals, oil pickup O-ring, coolant O-rings)
problems.recommendation: Despite the M113 being "one of the most reliable engines I've ever encountered", all of the seals are bad on these engines — valve stem seals, oil pickup O-ring, and front cover coolant O-rings. The oil pickup O-ring is a "single point of failure" that can destroy the engine if it fails. If your M113 is older, replace this O-ring preventatively. The rear main seal is also prone to failure. Check for oil spots regularly and investigate any unexplained oil consumption.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

problems.title: M113 Intake Manifold Flap Failure (R500 — 2006–2010)
problems.symptoms: Check engine light (P2005), loss of power, rough idle, poor fuel economy, intake manifold rattle
problems.recommendation: The M113's intake manifold has plastic flaps and actuator arms that can break. The common issue is that the intake manifold gums up with oil via the PCV system, causing the flaps to become sticky and the arms to break. Kits are available to fix this, but you also need to address the PCV system. If you see a P2005 code, have the intake manifold inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full intake manifold failure guide

problems.title: M273 Balance Shaft/Idler Gear Issues (R500 — 2010–2013)
problems.symptoms: Engine rattle on cold start, check engine light, camshaft correlation codes, rough running

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: early M273 engines share the M272's balance shaft/idler gear issues

problems.recommendation: The M273 V8 "will have the same problems with the cam shafts, causing repair costs to about $6,000 when about 100,000k is reached". The idler gear (which powers camshaft timing in the V8) suffers from the same soft metal issue as the M272's balance shaft. If you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This R350 / R500 Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * R500 with the M113 V8 (2006–2010) — widely regarded as one of Mercedes' most reliable engines
* R350 with revised balance shaft (later production) — the defining weakness addressed
* Evidence of oil seal replacement on M113 (valve stem seals, oil pickup O-ring) — the most important maintenance on the V8
* Full service history with regular oil changes (every 10,000 miles) — all three engines reward good maintenance
* Post-2010 R500 with documented timing chain/idler gear work — the M273's weakness addressed
avoidIf: * Pre-2010 R350 with no proof of balance shaft work — you're buying a known risk
* R350 with rattling or check engine light (P0016–P0019) — balance shaft failure is imminent
* R500 M113 with oil leaks and no service history — seals are failing
* R500 M273 with no proof of timing chain work — the M273 shares the M272's issues
* Poor service history — all three engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The R-Class petrols are powered by three very different engines. The M272 V6 (2006–2010) is the most problematic — balance shaft failure is a class-action-level issue. The M113 V8 (2006–2010) is widely regarded as one of Mercedes' most reliable engines, but oil seals and intake manifold flaps are well-known weaknesses. The M273 V8 (2010–2013) is more powerful but shares the M272's balance shaft/idler gear concerns. Buy the M113 if you can — it's the most reliable of the three. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and the R-Class will reward you with effortless cruising.

cta.label: → Compare R350 / R500 petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz R350 or R500 petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattling from the front of the engine (balance shaft/idler gear warning on M272 and M273)
2. Engine Number (R350) — Check if the balance shaft has been revised (pre-2010 cars are at risk)
3. Service History — including which engine (M272, M113 or M273) and evidence of major maintenance
4. Oil Leaks (R500 M113) — Any oil under the car or excessive oil consumption? (seals are failing)
5. Check Engine Light — Have codes been read? (P0016–P0019 indicate balance shaft issues; P2005 indicates intake manifold issues)
6. Intake Manifold Health (R500 M113) — Any loss of power or P2005 code? (flaps are failing)
7. Transmission — Any hard shifting? (7G-Tronic valve body failure is common)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — three engine families across the R-Class petrol's history.
